designs.angle: Operational CSEP consensus / hybrid ensembles. The proven prospective winners in the CSEP testing centers were NOT equal-weight pools of heterogeneous models; they were (a) Rhoades-style PAIRWISE multiplicative/additive HYBRIDS of an ETAS-family clustering model with a smoothed-seismicity background (Rhoades et al. 2014 BSSA, 2017 SRL, Rhoades & Gerstenberger 2009), and (b) SCORE-WEIGHTED LINEAR POOLS / BMA over a FAMILY OF ETAS VARIANTS that differ only in a few kernel/conditioning choices, with weights fit to the rolling prospective log-likelihood (Marzocchi, Zechar & Jordan 2012 BSSA 'BMA'; Taroni, Marzocchi, Schorlemmer et al. 2018 SRL Italy CSEP retrospective; Bayona et al. 2021/2022 GJI global ensembles; Serafini et al. 2022 for ETAS-mixture stacking). The SUP (Stationary+Uniform+Poisson) construction (Werner/Helmstetter style) and the Italy/NZ/California experiments all confirm: the ensemble beats its best component by a small, stable margin ONLY when (i) the components are individually well-calibrated ETAS-family rate fields sharing the same support, (ii) one component is the mandatory smoothed-seismicity floor so the pool never starves, and (iii) weights are learned from past prospective scores, not set equal. E12 mirrors (b) exactly with our tiled-ETAS family: an ensemble of ~4-5 tiled-ETAS VARIANTS (not heterogeneous model classes) plus the floor, combined by log-score-optimal convex stacking on a rolling holdout. This is the single lever deep research flagged as the only proven prospective gain (~+0.016 IGPE), and the CSEP-consensus construction is how we make that gain real and stable rather than the E8-E9 dilution failure.

recommended_variants.name: V0_base_tiled_etas (the anchor — must remain the dominant-weight component)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: The existing TiledForecaster as-is: tile_deg=10, halo_deg=1, min_events_for_etas=30, use_regime_priors=True, per-tile MLE n_restarts=0, max_parent_days=730, max_parent_dist_km=500. This is models['primary'] and the reference. Stacking is anchored so V0 carries the prior weight; the others are admitted only if they earn log-score on the holdout.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: It is the reference itself; the orthogonality requirement is on the OTHERS relative to it. Including V0 guarantees the pool can never score worse than base by more than the stacking estimation error (the convex hull contains the all-weight-on-V0 vertex).
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Mirrors the CSEP rule that the consensus model must contain the current best single model as a vertex (Marzocchi-Zechar-Jordan 2012; Italy CSEP, Taroni et al. 2018). The base ETAS-family model was the prospective winner in EarthquakeNPP (2024) and in CSEP-Italy among individual models.

recommended_variants.name: V1_short_memory_high_omori (recent-cluster-sharpened ETAS variant)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: Same TiledForecaster but with a productivity/decay regime tuned to recent triggering: tighten per-tile ETAS Omori bounds toward faster decay (p bound center raised, e.g. p in [1.0,1.5] but seed/prior nearer 1.2-1.3) and shorten max_parent_days from 730 to 120 and max_parent_dist_km from 500 to 250. Concretely a TiledForecaster(max_parent_days=120, max_parent_dist_km=250) feeding ETAS with a recent-weighted parent set.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Fails differently in TIME: it over-forecasts immediately after a mainshock and under-forecasts long aftershock tails, exactly where V0 (long 730d memory) is over-smoothed. During vigorous sequences V1 wins; in quiescence it reverts to background. So its log-score residuals are negatively correlated with V0's during the windows that matter (post-mainshock days), which is where stacking extracts gain.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Rhoades & Gerstenberger (2009) and Rhoades et al. (2014) ETAS+EEPAS hybrids showed the short-term clustering component and the long-memory component each won in disjoint regimes; the hybrid beat both. Helmstetter-Werner short-term ETAS vs long-memory smoothed in CSEP-California (Zechar et al. 2013) confirms the time-scale split.

recommended_variants.name: V2_long_memory_broad_smoothing (background-leaning ETAS variant = the SUP-style stationary anchor)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: TiledForecaster with use_regime_priors=True but broadened: larger smoothed-seismicity neighbour count (broader adaptive kernel), min_events_for_etas raised to ~60 so more tiles fall back to the stationary smoothed floor, and longer max_parent_days=730 kept but parent influence down-weighted. Effectively this is the smoothed-seismicity-dominated member that supplies the mandatory floor as a FULL rate field, not a tiny additive term.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Fails differently in SPACE/regime: it is the calibrated stationary background that wins in the long quiescent inter-sequence periods and in data-thin interiors where V0/V1's triggering sum is noise. This is the CSEP 'mandatory smoothed null as a real ensemble member' — it never starves the pool and dominates weight exactly when triggering models have no signal. The E8-E9 dead-end was including the null at EQUAL weight; here its weight is LEARNED and small except where it earns it.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Werner et al. (2011) and Helmstetter et al. (2007) smoothed-seismicity was the prospective winner in CSEP-California long-term; the SUP ensemble (Stationary+Uniform+Poisson) and Bayona et al. (2021, GJI) global ensembles required the smoothed background as a member for stable calibration. Marzocchi et al. (2012) BMA gave it nonzero but data-driven weight.

recommended_variants.name: V3_b_value_and_Mc_perturbed (GR-tail / completeness variant)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: TiledForecaster identical to V0 but with the global b_value and mc set to a perturbed pair rather than the per-tile estimate: e.g. b fixed at 1.20 and Mc=5.10 (vs base b≈1.337, Mc=5.35). Pass b_value=1.20, mc=5.10 to the constructor so every tile's GR exceedance fraction and productivity normalization shift.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Fails differently in MAGNITUDE: the GR-tail fraction gr_exceedance_fraction(M*) is exponentially sensitive to b. A lower b inflates the M>=6/M>=7 tail relative to M>=5. So V3's errors are concentrated at the high-threshold bands where V0 may be mis-calibrated, and its weight will be picked up specifically when large-event scoring rewards a heavier tail. b-value uncertainty is a genuine epistemic axis CSEP ensembles average over.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Taroni et al. (2018, SRL) Italy CSEP showed b-value and Mc choices materially changed per-model scores and the ensemble averaged the epistemic spread; Marzocchi & Sandri (2003) on b-uncertainty. Serafini et al. (2022) ETAS Bayesian mixtures explicitly integrate over magnitude-distribution parameters.

recommended_variants.name: V4_finer_tiling (spatial-resolution variant — optional 5th member, admit only if holdout supports it)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: TiledForecaster(tile_deg=5.0, halo_deg=1.0) — half the tile size, so per-tile ETAS is fit on smaller, more locally homogeneous halos (more tiles, more null-fallbacks in thin tiles). Everything else equal to V0.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Fails differently in SPATIAL RESOLUTION: finer tiles sharpen rate gradients along narrow plate boundaries (wins where seismicity is a thin lineament) but increase variance and null-fallback rate in sparse tiles (loses in diffuse interiors). Its skill is anti-correlated with V0 along subduction arcs vs continental interiors.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Rhoades et al. (2017, SRL) and the NZ National Seismic Hazard ensemble found grid resolution was a real model-uncertainty axis; CSEP regularizes resolution by spatial-bin testing (S-test). Keep V4 only if it earns positive holdout weight — it is the most likely member to be pruned to zero, which is the correct CSEP behaviour.

designs.weighting_method: Log-score-optimal convex stacking over the simplex (stacked density combination, Smyth-Wolpert 1998; CSEP/seismicity adaptation Serafini et al. 2022, Bayona et al. 2021-2022, Marzocchi-Zechar-Jordan 2012 BMA as the Bayesian sibling), with L2/Dirichlet shrinkage anchoring the weight vector to the base-tiled-ETAS vertex. Implemented as EnsembleForecaster.fit_weights_from_history producing the global convex weights w for its existing linear opinion pool.
designs.weight_fitting_math: Objective = maximize the pooled prospective Poisson log-likelihood of the linear-pool rate on a rolling holdout, equivalently minimize the negative log-score. For each holdout issue-date d in the trailing window W (e.g. the most recent ~180-365 issue days strictly before t_issue), each horizon h, threshold M*, and each fit cell i, let lambda_{k,i}(d,h,M*) be variant k's expected count and omega_i(d,h,M*) the observed count (events binned to nearest cell, exactly as backanalysis._bin_counts_to_cells). The pool rate is L_i = sum_k w_k lambda_{k,i}. Poisson log-score per cell = omega_i*log(L_i) - L_i (drop the omega! constant). Solve:  minimize over w in simplex  J(w) = -sum_{d,h,M*,i} [ omega_i*log(sum_k w_k*lambda_{k,i}) - sum_k w_k*lambda_{k,i} ]  +  (tau/2)*||w - e_0||^2  , where e_0 is the unit vector on V0 (shrinkage anchor) and tau is a small ridge chosen by nested rolling CV. J is CONVEX in w: the -omega*log(linear) term is convex (negative log of an affine-positive function), the +linear term is linear, the ridge is convex, and the simplex is convex — so there is a unique global optimum, solvable by projected gradient / exponentiated-gradient (multiplicative weights, EG) or a 5-variable SLSQP with sum-to-one equality and nonnegativity bounds (the repo already uses scipy for the ETAS MLE, so scipy.optimize.minimize with SLSQP is the drop-in). The EG update is preferable for stability on the simplex: w_k <- w_k * exp(eta * g_k) / Z with g_k = sum (omega_i*lambda_{k,i}/L_i - lambda_{k,i}) the per-component score gradient. Critically: weights are scored on the SAME IGPE log-score that backanalysis reports, so the stacking objective and the evaluation metric are the same functional (we are directly optimizing held-out information gain, not a proxy). Because the all-V0 vertex is in the feasible set and is the shrinkage anchor, the optimum satisfies J(w*) <= J(e_0): on the holdout the pool is provably no worse than base tiled ETAS; the prospective question is only whether that holdout edge generalizes to the next window (it does iff member skill is regime-stable, which V1-V3's regime-orthogonality is designed to ensure).
leakage_controls: Rolling-origin only: weights for issue date t are fit ONLY on holdout windows with both issue date AND target window end strictly before t (target end = d + h days < t). A 7-day horizon means the most recent 7 days before t are NOT yet fully observed and are excluded from the weight-fit holdout — otherwise the outcome leaks into the weights. Enforce target_end < t, not just d < t.

Reuse the existing forecast clock and assert_no_leakage: each variant V_k is conditioned by the SAME ForecastClock slice (-inf, t) the base model uses; the per-day variant rates are produced by the identical leakage-safe recondition path (TiledForecaster.recondition), so no variant sees the future. The stacking layer only consumes already-sealed, already-scored past forecasts.

Weights are re-solved on the weekly refit cadence (refit_every_days) using a frozen trailing holdout; they are HELD constant across the intra-cadence reconditioned days, exactly like the ETAS parameters — no daily peeking that could chase noise.

Score variants on the non-declustered target catalog (the dual-catalog rule is for inputs only) — same target as backanalysis, so the weight objective and the reported IGPE use the identical observed field.

Defence-in-depth: a unit assertion that the holdout set used by fit_weights_from_history has zero intersection with any (issue,horizon) pair whose target window overlaps [t, t+h). Add it next to assert_no_leakage so a regression is caught in CI, mirroring the existing temporal-leakage assertion.

Shrinkage anchor tau is itself selected by NESTED rolling CV inside the holdout (an inner rolling split), never tuned on the test windows that produce the reported IGPE — otherwise tau-selection leaks.

Variant hyperparameters (V1-V4 kernel/tiling/b choices) are FIXED a priori from CSEP literature, not grid-searched against the prospective score, so the only quantity fit to data is the 5-dim weight vector (low capacity → low overfit/selection risk).
designs.avoids_equal_weight_deadend: The E8-E9 dead-end was an EQUAL-WEIGHT (w_k=1/K) linear pool of HETEROGENEOUS model CLASSES {ETAS, smoothed-null, R-J}; averaging diluted the triggering signal because the stationary null and the weak R-J got 1/3 weight unconditionally regardless of whether they ever earned it. E12 differs on BOTH axes that caused the failure: (1) the members are tiled-ETAS VARIANTS of the SAME well-calibrated family (V0-V4) that all carry the triggering signal — there is no weak R-J class to dilute it; the smoothed background enters only as V2, a full calibrated rate field, not as a co-equal averaged term. (2) Weights are LEARNED by convex log-score stacking on a rolling holdout with an L2 shrinkage anchor on V0, so a member that does not improve held-out information gain is driven to ~zero weight (EG/SLSQP will zero out V4 if it does not earn it) and the pool collapses to base tiled ETAS in the worst case. Equal weighting is mathematically a single feasible point e=(1/K) that the convex program will only return if it is optimal — which it generically is not. The shrinkage-to-V0 anchor guarantees that with sparse holdout data we degrade to the base reference rather than to the harmful 1/K average. So E12 cannot reproduce the dilution failure: dilution requires fixed equal weight on a signal-free member, and here weight on any signal-free member is a free parameter that the held-out log-score sets to zero.
designs.eval_protocol: Run the SAME leakage-free pseudo-prospective back-analysis (run_back_analysis / global_backanalysis) that scores base tiled ETAS, with E12 as models['primary'] and base tiled ETAS as the comparison baseline, at horizons {1,2,7} days and thresholds {5.0,6.0,7.0}, daily issue cadence, weekly refit+restack cadence. Headline metric: per-horizon mean IGPE of E12 vs base tiled ETAS in nats (this is the deep-research +0.016-target lever) — report it as a NEW channel exactly parallel to the existing igpe_vs_etas channel, computed by csep.information_gain_per_earthquake(lam_ens, lam_base, omega). Decision rule (pre-registered, honest): E12 ships ONLY if (a) the 7-day prospective mean IGPE vs base is POSITIVE on a held-out forward period the weights never saw, AND (b) it is STABLE — positive (or non-negative within CI) in the majority of rolling windows, not driven by one sequence (report per-window IGPE distribution + a block-bootstrap / stationary-bootstrap CI over issue dates, blocking by aftershock sequence to respect temporal dependence), AND (c) it passes the CSEP consistency battery as well as base does: N-test, M-test, S-test, L-test pass rates not worse than base. Negative or unstable result is REPORTED as such (mandatory honesty) and E12 is not promoted — the 30-day ContextTPP non-generalization to 7-day is the cautionary precedent, so the 7-day prospective number is the gate, never a single-window 30-day win. Also report the learned weight trajectory over time (should be V0-dominant with small, regime-correlated mass on V1-V3) as an auditable artifact, and the fraction of windows where any member other than V0/V2 earns nonzero weight.
top_risks: The gain is real but tiny (~+0.016 IGPE per deep research) and may be statistically indistinguishable from zero at our global M>=5 event count over the available prospective window — the M>=5 catalog yields few independent target windows, so the block-bootstrap CI on 7-day IGPE may straddle zero. Mitigation: pool across thresholds/horizons for power, report CI honestly, and treat 'positive but not significant' as a non-ship per the pre-registered rule.

Member non-orthogonality: V1-V4 are all tiled ETAS, so their rate fields are highly correlated (corr often >0.9). Highly collinear members give stacking little to exploit and unstable weights. Mitigation: the variants are deliberately chosen on DIFFERENT failure axes (time V1, background/regime V2, magnitude-tail V3, spatial-resolution V4); monitor pairwise log-score-residual correlation and prune members whose residuals are not sufficiently orthogonal to V0 (they add variance, not gain).

Weight overfitting on a short holdout: 5 weights on few independent target windows can chase noise. Mitigated by L2 shrinkage to V0 and nested-CV tau, EG regularization, and global (not per-cell) weights — but if the holdout is very short the safe outcome is w≈e_0 (pool≈base) and NO gain, which is acceptable (no harm) but means E12 may simply equal base prospectively.

Regime non-stationarity: a member that won last year's windows (e.g. V1 during an active sequence) may be the wrong member next window; learned weights lag the regime. This is the same horizon/regime-dependence that killed the ContextTPP 30d->7d generalization. Mitigation: keep the trailing window long enough to span multiple sequences, anchor to V0, and accept that the stable expectation is a small average gain, not a large one.

Operational/compute cost: E12 runs ~5 tiled-ETAS fits per refit instead of 1 (~5x the weekly MLE sweep) plus the convex stack. Mitigation: variants share the tile partition and the smoothed background; reuse recondition daily; the stack is a 5-var convex solve (negligible). Still, verify the global weekly refit stays within the tractable end-to-end budget already established.

Floor / support mismatch: if a variant (e.g. V4 finer tiling) leaves more cells at the smoothed floor, the linear pool's per-cell support differs across members; ensure every member is floored to the SAME smoothed background before pooling (as backanalysis already floors lam_primary to lam_null) so floor differences don't masquerade as skill differences.

designs.angle: Treat E12 as Bayesian-flavored predictive stacking (Yao, Vehtari, Simpson, Gelman 2018, Bayesian Analysis 13:917; Burkner/Gabry/Vehtari 2021 leave-future-out) of the predictive DISTRIBUTIONS of a small set of ETAS-FAMILY variants, NOT a BMA over a discrete model index and NOT an equal-weight rate average. The objects pooled are per-cell Poisson predictive densities p_k(omega_i | lambda_{k,i}); the pooled predictive is the convex mixture-of-rates lambda_ens,i = sum_k w_k lambda_{k,i} on the probability simplex (linear opinion pool = a mixture of the component Poissons, which keeps P(>=1)=1-e^{-lambda} exact and the smoothed-null floor intact). Weights are chosen to maximize the realized leave-future-out Poisson log-score on the rolling pseudo-prospective holdout that the existing forecast-clock back-analysis already produces leakage-free. The whole design is dominated by ONE constraint surfaced directly by the code/experiments: M>=5 events are extremely sparse (E9: n_obs = 248 global, 11 CL, 5 JP, 2 NZ per 30-day window; E5: ~248 in 60 days global), so the number of free weights must be tiny (2-3 effective, global-pooled, not per-cell/per-region) and heavily regularized toward the ETAS vertex, or the weights overfit the handful of events and the win evaporates prospectively (exactly the H&M 2023 +0.016+/-0.028 story where the CI already straddles zero). The reference to beat is the single tiled ETAS vertex w=(1,0,0,...), so by construction the stack can never do WORSE than tiled ETAS on the fitting objective and the only open question is out-of-sample stability.

recommended_variants.name: V0_tiled_ETAS_base (the anchored vertex)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: The unchanged production TiledForecaster (regime-tiled ETAS, b=1.337, Mc=5.35, 195 ETAS tiles). It is component k=0 and the regularization target. Not a new model; it pins the simplex vertex w=(1,0,...) so the stack is provably >= tiled ETAS on the in-sample log-score.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: It is the reference; every other member must fail in places it does not, or it earns zero weight.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: E3/E5: single-window IGPE +4.65 vs null global, +0.072 Japan; it is the established backbone (improvement-evidence F-table lever 1 keeps ETAS-family as backbone).

recommended_variants.name: V1_long_memory_Omori (slow-decay aftershock variant)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: A second TiledForecaster instance with the Omori-Utsu temporal kernel constrained to the slow-decay corner: tighten DEFAULT_BOUNDS p to (0.8,1.1) and c to (1e-2,1.0) (vs base p in (0.8,1.5)). Same MLE, same two stability gates, same regime priors. Concretely: pass bounds={'p':(0.8,1.1),'c':(1e-2,1.0)} to the per-tile ETASForecaster via TiledForecaster.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: The base ETAS MLE tends to high p (fast decay) tuned to the median sequence; a long-memory member carries rate forward in the 2-7 day tail of long aftershock sequences (Japan-type) where the base under-forecasts late aftershocks. It is wrong (over-forecasts) in short, sharp sequences and in quiet cells — disjoint failure set from V0.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Serafini et al. 2025 prospective California archive is dominated by ETAS variants differing mainly in Omori parameters and ensembles of them; Herrmann & Marzocchi 2023 SMA members are ETAS_LM-type Omori variants. Long-horizon members are exactly the F6 horizon-specific lever (improvement-evidence).

recommended_variants.name: V2_isotropic_broad_spatial (wider aftershock-zone variant)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: A third TiledForecaster with the spatial kernel pushed to the broad/heavy-tail corner: gamma bound to (0.8,2.0) (faster zeta growth with M) and q to (1.1,1.8) (heavier spatial tail) vs base q in (1.1,3.0). bounds={'gamma':(0.8,2.0),'q':(1.1,1.8)}.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Base ETAS spatial kernel is fit tight; for large mainshocks the true aftershock zone is elongated/large and the base mis-places rate near the epicentre. The broad-spatial member spreads M>=5 offspring over a wider area, winning in the cells the base leaves cold around big events; it is over-diffuse (loses) in dense small-quake clusters. Disjoint from V0 and V1 (spatial vs temporal axis).
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Anisotropic/finite-fault spatial kernels are the ETAS-beats-vanilla-ETAS lever (experiments.md pending #5); isotropic-broad is the safe, fittable first step. CSEP California hybrids (Rhoades et al. 2014,2018) gain from spatial-kernel diversity.

designs.weighting_method: Convex predictive stacking on the probability simplex, weights global-pooled (one weight vector for the whole region, NOT per-cell or per-tile), with strong shrinkage toward the ETAS vertex. Implement as EnsembleForecaster.fit_weights_from_history: (1) reuse the existing forecast-clock back-analysis to produce, for a rolling holdout of issue dates t in a training window strictly BEFORE the evaluation period, the per-cell sealed rate matrix L[t] (cells x components) and the observed per-cell count vector omega[t]; (2) solve a single small convex program for w on the K-1 simplex (K=3-4 members) maximizing the summed Poisson log-score; (3) apply the weights as the existing linear pool lambda_ens = sum_k w_k lambda_k (the EnsembleForecaster already does exactly this once weights are set). Because M>=5 events are sparse, keep K<=4 and DO NOT fit per-region or per-cell weights; at most fit a SEPARATE weight vector per horizon (1d vs 2-7d), which is the only stratification the data can support (F6 horizon-specific weighting) and even that only when the per-horizon holdout has >~50 events — otherwise share one vector across horizons. Negative-control: if the holdout has < ~30 target events, skip fitting and return the ETAS vertex w=(1,0,..) (report 'insufficient events to fit weights, defaulting to tiled ETAS'), never a uniform pool (that is the E8/E9 dead-end).
designs.weight_fitting_math: Objective (the exact per-cell Poisson log-score the harness already computes in eval.csep.poisson_joint_log_likelihood): maximize over w on the simplex S = {w: w_k>=0, sum_k w_k = 1} the regularized realized leave-future-out log-score  Q(w) = sum_{t in holdout} sum_{i in cells} [ omega_{t,i} * ln(lambda_ens,t,i(w)) - lambda_ens,t,i(w) ]  -  (rho/2) * ||w - e_0||_2^2 , where lambda_ens,t,i(w) = sum_k w_k * L[t]_{i,k} is the mixture rate, omega_{t,i} the observed count, and e_0 = (1,0,...,0) the tiled-ETAS vertex; the -ln(omega!) term is dropped (constant in w). This Q is the sum of (concave log term) minus (linear term) minus (convex quadratic penalty), hence CONCAVE in w over the simplex => a unique global maximizer, solved as a small constrained convex problem (K=3-4 vars): either projected/mirror gradient ascent with simplex projection, an EM/multiplicative-weights fixed point (w_k <- w_k * [partial sum of omega*L_k/lambda_ens] / Z, the standard mixture-MLE update, which stays on the simplex automatically and needs no solver dependency), or scipy.optimize.minimize(SLSQP) with sum-to-one + bound constraints. rho is the shrinkage strength toward the ETAS vertex, chosen by an INNER rolling holdout (nested LFO) over a tiny grid rho in {0 (pure stacking), 1, 4, 16, 64} x N_events-scaled; pick the rho whose weights give the best score on a held-back slice of the training window, defaulting to LARGE rho (heavy shrinkage) when N_events is small so the stack collapses to ETAS rather than overfitting. Equivalent Dirichlet-prior reading: rho*||w-e_0||^2 is a log-prior pulling the stacking weights toward a point mass on ETAS (a regularized/pseudo-BMA stack, Yao et al. 2018 sec 3 'regularization of the simplex weights'); with rho->inf the stack IS tiled ETAS, with rho=0 it is pure log-score-optimal stacking. Effective-parameter budget: with K members you fit K-1 free weights; the rule of thumb (and the H&M 2023 cautionary CI) is >= ~20-30 target events per free weight, so K=3 (2 free weights) needs ~40-60 M>=5 events in the fitting window minimum, K=4 needs ~60-90 — which is why members are capped at 3-4 and weights are global-pooled.
leakage_controls: Weights are fit ONLY on issue dates strictly before the evaluation period: split the timeline into [fit-window | gap | evaluation-window]; fit_weights_from_history sees only the fit-window holdout, the back-analysis then scores the frozen-weight ensemble on the disjoint evaluation-window. The forecast clock's assert_no_leakage already guarantees each per-issue rate L[t] used in fitting was itself conditioned only on catalog (-inf, t).

Each component rate matrix L[t] is produced by the SAME ForecastClock + fit-on-cadence/recondition-daily path as production (backanalysis.run_back_analysis), so the rates the weights are fit on are the genuine sealed prospective rates, not retrospectively re-fit ones.
Nested holdout for rho selection (the hyperparameter) stays inside the fit-window: an inner LFO split so rho is not tuned on the evaluation period. Never select rho on the reported metric.

A temporal GAP (>= one aftershock-correlation length, e.g. 7-30 days) between fit-window and evaluation-window so a long aftershock sequence straddling the boundary cannot leak its label into the weights (leave-future-out with a buffer; Burkner et al. 2021).
Score the target on the NON-declustered catalog (the harness already does this) — the weights must be honest about clustering, the dual-catalog rule applies to inputs only.
Report weights AND per-component holdout log-scores in the manifest so a reviewer can audit that no member got weight without an in-sample reason.

Negative-control / honesty gate: also fit weights on a shuffled-label (time-permuted) holdout; if shuffled-fit weights score as well as real-fit weights on the evaluation window, declare NO real stacking signal and ship tiled ETAS.
designs.avoids_equal_weight_deadend: Three structural guarantees, each tied to the recorded E8/E9 failure. (1) The members are ETAS-FAMILY VARIANTS (V0 base + V1 long-memory Omori + V2 broad-spatial), NOT the heterogeneous {ETAS, smoothed-null, R-J} set whose averaging diluted the triggering signal in E9 — every member carries the triggering signal, the smoothed null appears ONLY as the per-tile background inside each ETAS and as the published floor, never as a pool member competing for weight. (2) Weights are LOG-SCORE-FITTED on a leakage-free holdout, not equal: the optimizer is free to (and on sparse data will) drive weights for weak members to ~0, so a member that does not help is automatically excluded — the opposite of the forced uniform average. (3) The objective is anchored at the tiled-ETAS vertex e_0 with shrinkage rho: Q(e_0) = single-tiled-ETAS log-score is always feasible, so the fitted stack is provably >= tiled ETAS IN-SAMPLE by construction, and with large rho on sparse data it collapses BACK to tiled ETAS rather than to the uniform pool. The uniform vertex w=(1/K,...) is never the default and is in fact penalized away from by the e_0-anchored prior. The honest expectation, set by H&M 2023 (+0.016+/-0.028, CI includes 0), is a SMALL gain that may not be significant — reported as such, with the negative-control shuffle as the kill-switch.
designs.eval_protocol: Score E12 exactly like any other forecaster through the existing pseudo-prospective back-analysis (eval.backanalysis.run_back_analysis) on the disjoint evaluation-window, leakage-free via the forecast clock. Headline metric: information_gain_per_earthquake (nats) of the frozen-weight stack vs the SINGLE tiled ETAS (the reference to beat) AND vs the smoothed null — both channels, per the existing igpe_vs_etas / igpe_vs_null record. Skill is claimed ONLY when igpe_vs_tiled_ETAS > 0 with the paired Rhoades-2011 T-test CI excluding zero on the positive side, corroborated by the W-test (comparison_tests already implements both); given the H&M prior, fully expect a CI that includes zero and report that honestly. Run it the same way E5 ran: multi-region (high-seismicity JP/CL/US-CA/NZ + low-seismicity US-CE/EU-W/AU-E + GLOBAL) and per-horizon (1/2/7 d), so the gain is shown to generalize across regimes, not cherry-picked on Japan. Also report the N/M/S/L consistency tests on the stack (it must remain calibrated, not just win the comparison). Promotion rule: E12 enters the public field ONLY if the prospective IGPE vs tiled ETAS is positive and the T-test CI excludes zero on at least the GLOBAL view AND it does not lose significantly anywhere; otherwise record the negative result (as E9 did) and keep tiled ETAS as primary. Compare the measured number against the literature prior (~+0.016 IGPE) in the write-up.
top_risks: Data sparsity dominates: with only ~248 global / single-digit regional M>=5 events per window, even 2 free weights can overfit; the H&M 2023 +0.016+/-0.028 CI already straddles zero, so the realistic outcome is a non-significant gain. Mitigation = K<=4, global-pooled weights, heavy e_0-shrinkage rho selected by nested LFO, and the shuffled-label kill-switch.

Member collinearity: the three ETAS variants share the same tiles, background, and most parameters, so their rate fields L_k are highly correlated => the log-score is nearly flat in w (ill-conditioned), weights are unstable run-to-run. Mitigation = report the condition number / effective number of distinct members, prefer fewer truly-orthogonal members, and let shrinkage regularize the flat directions.

Horizon/region stratification temptation: fitting separate weights per region or per (region x horizon) instantly blows the event budget and reintroduces overfitting. Hard rule = one global weight vector, at most split by horizon and only when that horizon's holdout has >~50 events.

Pseudo-prospective is not fully prospective: the gold-standard CSEP California archive (Serafini 2025) shows even good retrospective/pseudo wins are modest; our pseudo-prospective number is an upper-ish bound, so do not over-claim. Frame as 'pseudo-prospective, awaiting truly prospective accrual'.

Background-floor double-counting: because each member is floored to the smoothed null AND the pool is convex, a careless implementation could let the null floor dominate quiet cells and wash out the triggering differences exactly where they matter; keep the floor applied to lambda_ens once at publish, not inside each member's contribution to the score (mirror the backanalysis lam_primary = max(etas, null) handling so the floor difference is not scored as skill).

Optimizer/solver fragility on the simplex with near-zero rates: use the multiplicative-weights/EM mixture update (no external solver, stays on simplex, robust to lambda->0 via the eps-floor already in poisson_joint_log_likelihood) as the default, SLSQP only as a cross-check.
citations: Yao, Vehtari, Simpson & Gelman (2018), 'Using Stacking to Average Bayesian Predictive Distributions', Bayesian Analysis 13(3):917-1007 — the log-score-optimal simplex-stacking objective and its regularization, the core method for E12.

Burkner, Gabry & Vehtari (2021), 'Approximate leave-future-out cross-validation for Bayesian time series models', J. Stat. Comput. Simul. — the leave-future-out / temporal-gap holdout that makes the weight-fitting leakage-free for a forecast.

Herrmann & Marzocchi (2023), GJI 234:73 — the prospective anchor: score-model-averaging ETAS ensemble gains only +0.016+/-0.028 IGPE over the best single member (ETAS_LM); the CI-straddles-zero honesty prior for E12.
Marzocchi, Zechar & Jordan (2012), BSSA 102:2574 — the BMA / Bayes-factor weighting framework applied to the RELM California prospective test (the BMA contrast to pure stacking).
Rhoades, Gerstenberger et al. (2014, 2018) — multiplicative/additive hybrid ETAS ensembles in operational CSEP (member-diversity evidence for the variant choice).
Rhoades et al. (2011), Acta Geophysica 59:728 — information-gain-per-earthquake + paired T-test, the exact skill metric/test the harness uses to score E12.
Serafini et al. (2025), Nature Scientific Data — the decade-long fully-prospective CSEP California archive: ETAS variants + ETAS/STEP ensembles, no ML; the realism check on expected gains.
Ogata (1998), Ann. Inst. Statist. Math. 50:379 — the space-time ETAS whose kernels (Omori-Utsu p,c; spatial gamma,q) are perturbed to define the V1/V2 members.
Gneiting & Raftery (2007), JASA 102:359 — proper scoring rules: the Poisson log-score used as the stacking objective is strictly proper, which is what makes the fitted weights honest.

designs.angle: ETAS refinement variants with orthogonal prospective skill, convex-stacked by rolling prospective log-score. The base `primary` is a single TiledForecaster (one fixed ETAS configuration per ~195 regime tiles, 7 Ogata-1998 params, smoothed-null fallback). E12 = a small pool of TiledForecaster instances that share the tiling/halo/routing/stability machinery but differ ONLY in the per-tile ETAS kernel/parameterization/memory/window, combined as a linear opinion pool (lambda_ens = sum_k w_k lambda_k) whose weights are learned per-regime from the rolling prospective Poisson log-score on a strictly-past holdout. This is the only deep-research-supported prospective lever (score-weighted ETAS-FAMILY stacking, ~+0.016 IGPE; Taroni et al. 2018, Rhoades et al. 2014/2018 hybrids, CSEP Italy/NZ). It explicitly is NOT the E8-E9 dead-end: every pool member is an ETAS-family triggering model, the stationary smoothed null enters only as each member's mandatory background floor (never as a co-equal averaged component), and weights are score-fit not equal. Variants are chosen to FAIL IN DIFFERENT PLACES than the base so the convex combination has lower log-loss than any single member.

recommended_variants.name: V1 - Anisotropic / finite-fault aftershock kernel (large-mainshock spatial relocation)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: Replace the isotropic inverse-power spatial_density f(r|m) with an elongated kernel for parents above a magnitude switch (m_parent >= ~6.0): distance measured to a finite rupture proxy instead of the epicentre. Cheapest faithful version on our code: keep f(r|m)=(q-1)/(pi zeta^2)(1+r^2/zeta^2)^-q but (a) bump the spatial scale zeta(m)=D e^{gamma(m-M0)} with a magnitude-dependent gamma so the aftershock zone grows ~ rupture length L~10^{0.5m-1.85} km (Wells & Coppersmith 1994), and (b) for m>=6 parents compute r as distance to a line segment of length L oriented along the local PB2002 plate-boundary azimuth (already loaded by the regime enricher), not to the point. Implement as an ETASForecaster subclass overriding _triggering_field and conditional_intensity's distance computation; all 7-param MLE, closed-form Omori window integral, and stability gates are unchanged. New scalar params: m_switch, L-coeff; no new fitted dimension if azimuth comes from PB2002.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Base isotropic ETAS smears a megathrust's aftershocks as a symmetric blob centred on the epicentre; it is systematically WRONG along the strike of a long rupture (under-forecasts at the rupture ends, over-forecasts perpendicular). V1 fails where the base is right (small/moderate events, where isotropy is fine and the segment proxy adds noise) and wins exactly where the base loses the most log-score: the days/weeks after an M>=6.5-7 mainshock on an elongated subduction interface. Since those rare large-mainshock windows dominate the IGPE numerator (IGPE is per-earthquake and aftershock-rich windows carry most events), a member that is better THERE and equal elsewhere is the highest-value orthogonal addition.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Finite-fault / anisotropic ETAS spatial kernels are an established prospective improvement: Ogata (1998) inverse-power; Hainzl et al. (2008) and Bach & Hainzl (2012, GJI) show fault-geometry-aware aftershock density improves likelihood; the ETAS-incorporating-finite-source line (Guo et al. 2015) and CSEP Italy hybrids using smoothed-seismicity-along-faults (Rhoades et al. 2014) all report positive prospective gain. Anisotropy is the single most physically defensible orthogonal kernel change. RETROSPECTIVE-ONLY caveat: rupture azimuth from PB2002 is a static proxy, not the true finite-fault solution, so the gain is a lower bound; the full finite-fault inversion is not available in real time and must NOT be used.

recommended_variants.name: V2 - Spatially-varying (per-tile MLE) vs regime-pooled-prior productivity & decay
recommended_variants.concrete_change: Run a second TiledForecaster with use_regime_priors flipped to a STRONGER empirical-Bayes shrinkage: keep K, c, p, D, gamma shrunk hard to the Page et al. 2016 regime centres (RegimePrior) and let only the local data move alpha and the background. Concretely: add a `prior_shrinkage` weight to ETASForecaster that adds a Gaussian penalty 0.5*tau*sum((theta-theta_prior)^2) to _negative_loglik for (K,c,p,D,gamma), with tau set so a thin tile (n<~80) is essentially the regime prior and a dense tile (n>~500) is essentially the free MLE. Base uses tau=0 (prior only seeds the start). One new scalar hyperparam tau; the MLE, gates, closed-form counts, and recondition path are otherwise identical.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: The base free-MLE 7-param fit is noisy on thin tiles (intraplate interiors, ridges, fresh tiles with <100 events) -> it overfits a local Omori p or productivity K to a handful of events and over/under-forecasts the next window. V2 fails on the DENSE high-information tiles (subduction interfaces with thousands of events, where shrinkage is a bias that throws away real local structure) but wins on the LONG TAIL of sparse tiles that cover most of the globe's area. Base and V2 therefore fail in complementary regions of the tile population; per-regime stacking weight will load V2 in sparse regimes and the base in dense regimes -> a strictly-better convex mixture by construction.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Empirical-Bayes / borrow-strength shrinkage of regional ETAS toward global tectonic-regime aftershock statistics is exactly the USGS Aftershock Forecaster (OAF) design (Page et al. 2016, BSSA; Hardebeck et al. 2019 USGS OAF) and is prospectively validated operationally. CSEP consensus (Taroni et al. 2018, SRL, CSEP Italy 10-yr) is that pooled/regularized models are more robust out-of-sample than free local fits. Shrinkage strength tau is the only thing that needs a holdout choice -> set it on the SAME rolling-past holdout used for the weights (no leakage).

recommended_variants.name: V3 - Longer-memory Omori (forecast-tuned c,p) vs the base likelihood-fit decay
recommended_variants.concrete_change: A TiledForecaster whose per-tile Omori is NOT free-MLE'd but fixed to a longer-memory pair (lower p ~1.05-1.08, slightly larger c) per regime, OR equivalently widen the bounds and add a mild prior pulling p down. Mechanically: set DEFAULT_BOUNDS p to a narrower low-p band per regime (e.g. crustal p in [0.95,1.10]) and raise max_parent_days from 730 to ~1825 (5 yr) so slowly-decaying sequences keep contributing. Everything else identical; this is a config/bounds change plus the memory cutoff, no new code path.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: The base MLE p is fit to maximize IN-SAMPLE likelihood over the whole training window, which is dominated by the dense early-aftershock cluster -> it tends to a steeper p (fast decay) that under-forecasts the slowly-decaying late-aftershock tail in the 1-7 day prospective window after older mainshocks. V3 deliberately over-weights long memory: it OVER-forecasts during quiet periods and right after a mainshock (where the base's faster decay is correct) but UNDER-loses during the extended decay of months-old sequences that are still live in the catalog. The base and V3 bracket the true decay; a convex weight between them is a forecast-tuned effective p that pure in-sample MLE cannot reach.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: p and c are the parameters most prone to in-sample-vs-prospective mismatch: Omori-Utsu p estimates are systematically window-dependent (Utsu et al. 1995). ETAS forecast competitions repeatedly find that decay tuned to the forecast horizon beats the likelihood-optimal decay (Zhuang 2011; Nandan et al. 2019, JGR, show prospective ETAS skill is sensitive to the temporal kernel). The 5-yr memory extension is supported by long aftershock sequences (e.g. post-2011 Tohoku) still being productive years later. This variant is the lowest implementation cost (config + cutoff).

recommended_variants.name: V4 - Magnitude-dependent productivity correction (alpha vs the b-value relation) - secondary
recommended_variants.concrete_change: A TiledForecaster variant that fixes alpha to a regime value tied to beta (e.g. alpha = 0.9*beta, i.e. productivity scaling locked just below the finite-branching limit, the alpha~b ln10 physical expectation) instead of free-MLE alpha. Add an `alpha_mode` to ETASForecaster: 'free' (base) vs 'tied' (alpha = kappa*beta, kappa~0.8-0.9 from regime). Pure config + a one-line constraint in _maximize_likelihood (drop alpha from the optimizer vector when tied). Gates unchanged (tied alpha is subcritical by construction).
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Free-MLE alpha is the most unstable of the 7 params on thin/quiet tiles and frequently rails to its bound or to a value that mis-scales how much a large vs small parent triggers. The base over/under-weights big-mainshock productivity tile-by-tile; V4's tied alpha gives a consistent, physically-bounded productivity scaling that fails on tiles with genuinely anomalous productivity (volcanic/swarm ridges) but is steadier everywhere else. Orthogonal to V2 (which shrinks K,c,p,D,gamma but frees alpha) - V4 is the complementary half (frees the rest, fixes alpha).
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: alpha~beta=b ln10 is the long-standing physical expectation (Helmstetter 2003; Hainzl et al. 2013) and tied-alpha ETAS is used when data are too sparse for a stable free alpha (USGS OAF generic params, Page et al. 2016). Lower implementation cost than V1; rank it 4th because its gain overlaps partly with V2's shrinkage. Flag: keep V4 OUT of the initial pool if V2 already carries the sparse-tile regime, to avoid two near-collinear members diluting the stack.

recommended_variants.name: V5 - Longer training window (full 1990-2026) vs shorter recent-window fit - tie-breaker
recommended_variants.concrete_change: A TiledForecaster fit on a SHORTER recent training slice (e.g. last 8-10 yr before t_issue) as a pool member alongside the base full-catalog (1990-2026) fit. Implement by passing a `train_lookback_days` to the tiled fit that pre-slices the conditional catalog to [t_issue - lookback, t_issue) before tiling. The full-window base captures long-term productivity; the short-window member tracks recent rate changes / catalog-completeness improvements. Lowest code cost (a catalog slice before fit).
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: The full-window base is biased by decades-old, less-complete, differently-located seismicity and is slow to react to a recently-activated zone; the short-window member is reactive but noisy and misses the long-term background productivity. They fail on opposite timescales: base under-reacts to new activation, short over-reacts to recent flukes. A convex weight is an effective exponential-forgetting fit the base cannot express.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Training-window sensitivity is well documented (Seif et al. 2017, BSSA, on ETAS parameter stability vs catalog length; Nandan et al. 2019). CSEP forecasters routinely combine long- and short-memory variants. RANK LAST: gain is the least reliable and most overlaps V3's memory effect; include only if the holdout shows V3 is not already capturing the timescale spread. Use as a tie-breaker member, not a core one.

designs.weighting_method: Per-regime convex (linear opinion pool) stacking weights learned from the rolling pseudo-prospective Poisson log-score. NOT one global weight vector and NOT equal weights. Five (or however many regimes are populated) weight simplices, one per TectonicRegime, because the variants' relative skill is regime-dependent by design (V1 wins on subduction interfaces, V2/V4 on sparse intraplate/ridge, V3/V5 on extended sequences). Each tile inherits its dominant-regime weight vector (already computed by dominant_regime in tiled.py), so the per-cell ensemble rate is lambda_ens(cell) = sum_k w_{regime(cell),k} * lambda_k(cell), reusing the existing EnsembleForecaster.expected_counts machinery but with regime-routed weights. The mandatory smoothed null is NOT a pool member; it stays each member's mu(x,y) background floor and the cold-start floor applied downstream, so the floor is identical across members and never enters the stacked weights as a separate diluting component (this is the precise structural fix vs the E8-E9 equal-weight pool). Weights are recomputed on the SAME weekly full-refit cadence as the model family (configs/publish.yaml train_cadence.full_refit), held across the daily recondition days, and persisted in the manifest for auditability.
designs.weight_fitting_math: Objective: minimize the pooled negative Poisson log-score (= the L-test/IGPE kernel, so optimizing weights optimizes exactly the metric we are scored on) over a rolling strictly-past holdout, separately per regime, subject to a convex-combination constraint. For regime g, let the holdout be the set of (issue date d, cell i in a tile of regime g, horizon H, threshold M*) tuples with d in [t_issue - W_holdout, t_issue) (W_holdout ~ 180-365 days, all strictly before the current fit's t_issue). For each member k, lambda_{k,d,i} is that member's expected count (computed by the SAME forecast clock, conditioned only on (-inf, d), then scored against the observed omega_{d,i} in [d, d+H)). Solve:  min_{w in simplex} sum_{d,i} [ (sum_k w_k lambda_{k,d,i}) - omega_{d,i} * ln(sum_k w_k lambda_{k,d,i} + eps) ]  s.t. w_k >= 0, sum_k w_k = 1. This is a convex problem (Poisson NLL is convex in the rate, the rate is linear in w, the simplex is convex) -> a unique global optimum, solved with scipy.optimize.minimize(method='SLSQP') with the linear equality + bound constraints, or projected gradient. Regularization to avoid a corner solution on a short/quiet holdout: add a small ridge/entropic prior lambda_reg * sum_k (w_k - 1/K)^2 (or -lambda_reg*sum w_k ln w_k) and floor weights so each member keeps w_k >= w_min (~0.05); lambda_reg and w_min are themselves fixed a-priori (not tuned on the test) to keep the stack honest. Degenerate-holdout fallback: if regime g has < n_min observed earthquakes in the holdout (sparse regimes, common), fall back to equal weights among ETAS members OR to w=1 on the base TiledForecaster (never below the base) - this guarantees E12 >= base in expectation on data-starved regimes and means a no-signal regime cannot drag the global score below the reference. Optimal-Poisson-weight identity: at the optimum the gradient condition sum_{d,i} lambda_{k,d,i}*(omega/(sum_j w_j lambda_j) - 1) = 0 for all in-support k recovers the standard EM/multiplicative-weight update for lambda_pool, so an EM inner loop is an equivalent, derivative-free solver if SLSQP is unavailable.
leakage_controls: The stacking holdout is strictly in the past: weights for the forecast issued at t_issue are fit ONLY on (issue, score) pairs whose entire target window [d, d+H) ends before t_issue. No member forecast and no observed omega used to fit weights ever overlaps the window E12 is currently forecasting.

Each member's per-holdout-date lambda is produced by the EXISTING ForecastClock + recondition path (inference/clock.py + backanalysis.py), which already conditions on (-inf, d) and re-asserts assert_no_leakage(past, d). Weight-fitting consumes those clock outputs; it adds no new data access, so it inherits the harness's leakage proof.

Weights are refit on the weekly full-refit cadence and HELD (not refit) across daily recondition days, exactly like the ETAS parameters - so a daily forecast never re-peeks at its own near-future to retune weights.

lambda_reg, w_min, W_holdout, n_min, and the m_switch/L-coeff/tau/kappa member hyperparameters are FIXED a priori (or chosen on a separate, older development period that is itself disjoint from every evaluation window) - they are NOT grid-searched on the back-analysis period that produces the published IGPE, which would be a second, subtler leak.

The final published IGPE is computed by the unchanged backanalysis driver treating E12 as just another `primary` forecaster scored against the smoothed null AND catalog-only single tiled ETAS - E12 must beat the base tiled ETAS as a separate baseline, so the comparison is the same code that scores every other model (no special-casing).

Member forecasts feeding the holdout are cached keyed on (member_id, issue_date, config_hash) so refitting weights does not recompute clock steps, but the cache key includes t_issue and the catalog hash - a cache entry can only be reused for a date strictly before the current forecast, never for a future date.
designs.avoids_equal_weight_deadend: The E8-E9 dead-end failed for two structural reasons, both removed here. (1) It averaged HETEROGENEOUS models {ETAS, smoothed-null, R-J}: the stationary smoothed null and the weak R-J have near-zero triggering signal, so equal-weighting them diluted ETAS triggering rate exactly where triggering is the predictable signal (active sequences) - a 1/3 weight on a flat null drags the post-mainshock rate down by ~3x. E12 pool members are ALL ETAS-family triggering models with the same smoothed null as their shared mu(x,y) background; the null is a floor inside every member, never a co-equal averaged term, so it cannot dilute the triggering signal. (2) It used EQUAL weights, optimal only if every component is equally skilled - the deep-research finding is that the smoothed null is far less skilled, so equal weighting is provably sub-optimal. E12 learns convex weights by minimizing the exact prospective log-score, so a less-skilled member (or a whole regime with no signal) is automatically down-weighted toward zero, and the degenerate-holdout fallback floors the stack at w=1 on the base tiled ETAS. Therefore E12 >= base tiled ETAS in expectation on the fitting metric: the base is in the feasible set (w = e_base), and the convex optimum is no worse than any feasible point on the holdout; out-of-sample it can only lose by the usual stacking generalization gap, which the past-only holdout + ridge/entropic regularization + w_min floor are sized to control. This is precisely why the gain is expected to be SMALL but POSITIVE and STABLE (~+0.01-0.02 IGPE, matching Taroni et al. 2018 / Rhoades hybrids), not a large jump - a large jump would itself be a red flag for leakage.
designs.eval_protocol: Leakage-free pseudo-prospective CSEP back-analysis via the existing run_back_analysis driver, with E12 slotted as `primary` and scored on the NON-declustered target catalog over [t, t+H). Headline metric: mean information_gain_per_earthquake (nats) of E12 vs the base single TiledForecaster, added as an EXPLICIT second baseline channel (igpe_vs_base_tiled) alongside the existing igpe_vs_null and igpe_vs_etas - skill is claimed ONLY if this is positive AND the paired Rhoades-2011 T-test CI excludes zero on the positive side AND the W-test corroborates (csep.comparison_tests), per the existing skill rule. Report at horizons 1/2/7 days and thresholds M5/6/7 INDEPENDENTLY and report ALL cells including the ones where E12 does not beat the base (no post-hoc selection - the recorded honesty rule). Run the global back-analysis (global_backanalysis.py, coarse 1.0deg world grid) over a multi-year period that is DISJOINT from the development period used to fix hyperparameters. Also report N/M/S/L consistency per the existing harness (calibration, necessary-not-sufficient). Sanity gate before believing any positive result: (a) confirm E12 reduces to the base when the holdout is empty/quiet (weights -> e_base); (b) confirm the per-regime weight vectors are not all railed to a corner (a sign of an under-regularized or leaking holdout); (c) confirm the gain is concentrated in the aftershock-rich windows the orthogonal variants target, not spread implausibly into quiet cells (which would indicate a scoring artifact). Negative or non-significant result is reported as such.
top_risks: Collinearity of pool members: V2, V4, V5 partially overlap (all are regularize/restrict the fit); a near-collinear pool makes the stacking weights unstable and adds no orthogonal skill. Mitigation: start with the 3 most orthogonal members (base, V1 anisotropic, V3 long-memory) + V2 sparse-tile shrinkage; add V4/V5 only if the holdout log-score strictly improves and the weight vector stays in the simplex interior.

Sparse-regime holdout starvation: M>=5 events are rare per regime over a past holdout, so the per-regime Poisson log-score has high variance and the weight optimum is noisy -> overfit weights that lose out-of-sample. Mitigation: the equal-weight/base-fallback below n_min observed earthquakes, the w_min floor, the ridge/entropic regularizer, and pooling thresholds (use M>=5 events to fit weights even if forecasting M>=5/6/7) - all sized a priori.

The expected gain (~+0.016 IGPE) is at or below the noise floor of the global back-analysis, so the T-test CI may not exclude zero even if the mean is positive -> an honest-but-null headline. This is the deep-research-predicted reality, not a bug; budget for reporting a small, possibly-non-significant gain and do not tune to manufacture significance (that is the leak).

Compute cost: stacking requires running K members through the clock over the holdout AND the evaluation period. Mitigation: members share the tiling, routing cache, smoothed background, and recondition path; only the per-tile ETAS kernel differs, so V3/V4/V5 are config-only and add ~0 fit cost, and V1/V2 add one extra per-tile MLE each. Cache member forecasts keyed on (member, issue_date, config_hash).

Implementing fit_weights_from_history on EnsembleForecaster but feeding it heterogeneous components again (re-introducing the smoothed null/R-J as pool members) would silently recreate the E8-E9 dead-end. Guardrail: the ensemble builder for E12 must assert every component is an ETAS-family TiledForecaster variant and that the smoothed null is present ONLY as each member's background, not as a component - enforce in build code + a unit test.

Anisotropic V1 azimuth from PB2002 is a static proxy, not a real-time finite-fault solution; for a mainshock on a curved or conjugate structure the assumed strike can be wrong and V1 can be WORSE than isotropic there. The convex weight protects the pool (V1 gets down-weighted if it hurts on the holdout), but flag V1's gain as a lower bound and never publish the azimuth as a fault solution.

designs.angle: Adversarial-skeptic / fail-safe stacking. E12 must be a convex (weights >= 0, sum 1) score-weighted pool of ETAS-FAMILY VARIANTS ONLY, with weights fit exclusively on the strictly-past rolling log-score via the forecast clock, and a hard-wired structural guarantee that the base TiledForecaster (the reference to beat) is always a member and the optimizer CANNOT do worse than it in-sample. The honest claim is small (~+0.016 IGPE, Herrmann and Marzocchi 2023), so the entire design is built to detect when there is NO real gain and record E12 as a dead-end rather than ship a retrospective mirage.

recommended_variants.name: V0_base_tiled (anchor, MANDATORY member)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: The existing TiledForecaster at its fitted params (b=1.337, Mc=5.35, ~195 tiles, max_parent_dist_km=500, max_parent_days=730). Unchanged. It is pinned into the pool and the weight simplex always includes it.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: It is the reference. Including it makes the pool a strict superset of the base: the convex-weight optimum is >= base log-score in-sample by construction (put all mass on V0). Any prospective loss is then ONLY weight-estimation noise, not a worse model class — this is the structural property E8-E9 lacked.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: It is our own best single model (E3: +4.65 nats self-consistency; E5: +0.072 Japan 1d prospective). Herrmann and Marzocchi 2023 GJI 234:73 confirm the SMA ensemble only edges the best single ETAS member by +0.016+/-0.028 — so the base must be IN the pool, not replaced.

recommended_variants.name: V1_short_memory_tiled (fast-Omori)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: Clone of the tiled fit with the triggering memory shortened: max_parent_days 730 -> 120 and a steeper Omori p re-MLE'd per tile (start p at base+0.15, bound p in [1.0,1.4]). Everything else identical, refit on the same lawful past.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Wins during the first days of vigorous aftershock sequences where recent triggering dominates and the long 2-yr memory dilutes the signal; loses in quiet inter-sequence periods. Its error is anticorrelated with V2 (long-memory), so the pool gains where V0's single fixed p is a compromise.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: Italy pseudo-prospective (improvement-evidence F6 / Marzocchi 2023): no single ETAS parameterization dominates all horizons; short-term skill is maximized by faster-decay triggering. RELM/CSEP ETAS-variant pools (Serafini 2025 Nature Sci Data archive, all-ETAS) are built from exactly such kernel variants.

recommended_variants.name: V2_long_memory_tiled (slow-Omori / secondary triggering)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: Clone with max_parent_days 730 -> 1825 (5 yr) and Omori p bounded lower [0.9,1.1], productivity K start raised ~10%. Captures long secondary-aftershock tails and swarms.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Wins on protracted swarms and late aftershocks (months-out triggering) where V1 has already decayed to background; fails by over-forecasting in the days right after a mainshock. Opposite failure mode to V1, so stacking the two brackets the true decay the single base p cannot.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: ETAS secondary-triggering / long-memory variants are standard CSEP members (Ogata 1998; the OEF-Italy ETES/STEP+ETAS SMA pool, Marzocchi 2023). The point of the pool is bracketing decay uncertainty, not a single MLE compromise.

recommended_variants.name: V3_wide_kernel_tiled (anisotropy proxy / spatial robustness)
recommended_variants.concrete_change: Clone with max_parent_dist_km 500 -> 800 and the spatial smoothing D start raised (gamma magnitude-scaling start +0.1), so large-event aftershock zones spread wider. Isotropic but broader — a cheap stand-in for the finite-fault kernel (experiments.md pending item 5) without new code.
recommended_variants.why_orthogonal: Wins for M>=6.5 mainshocks whose true aftershock zone exceeds the 500 km isotropic reach (off-fault and along-strike events that the base under-places); negligible/slightly-diluting for the abundant M5-6 events. Its gains land on a different, rarer event subset than V1/V2 (which are temporal), so it is near-orthogonal in the score covariance.
recommended_variants.prospective_evidence: FERN/RECAST gains over ETAS came largely from spatial-kernel flexibility, and they only beat an isotropic-kernel (weak-baseline) ETAS (improvement-evidence F4, Zlydenko 2023). Encoding a wider/anisotropy-proxy kernel as a POOL MEMBER captures that lever without adopting a neural model that has no prospective win (F3).

designs.weighting_method: Convex (non-negative, sum-to-1) log-score-optimal stacking weights over the four ETAS-family variants, fit ONLY on a rolling strictly-past window of daily forecast-clock log-scores, refit on the same weekly cadence the back-analysis already uses (refit_every_days=7), with the base V0_base_tiled pinned into the simplex so the pool is a strict superset of the base. Not Bayesian-posterior weights (those over-trust in-sample fit and the E8-E9 averaging would recur); explicit out-of-sample log-score stacking in the sense of Yao et al. 2018 (Bayesian Analysis 13:917, 'Using Stacking to Average Bayesian Predictive Distributions'), which is the held-out analogue of the Marzocchi-Zechar-Jordan 2012 BMA / inverse-log-likelihood SMA weighting that OEF-Italy and Herrmann and Marzocchi 2023 use operationally.
designs.weight_fitting_math: Components produce per-cell rates lambda_k(i,t) (k=1..4 variants). Pool rate lambda_w(i,t)=sum_k w_k lambda_k(i,t), w in simplex {w>=0, sum w=1}. At each weekly refit date t0 the weights solve a Poisson log-score stacking problem over the TRAILING window W of daily issues s in [t0 - L, t0) (L = 180 days default), each scored against its OWN realized target (leakage-free because every s < t0 and each forecast was conditioned on (-inf,s)):  maximize_w  sum_{s in W} sum_i [ -lambda_w(i,s) + omega_i(s) ln lambda_w(i,s) ]  subject to w>=0, sum w=1.  This is the exact same Poisson joint log-likelihood already implemented (csep.poisson_joint_log_likelihood); it is concave in w (log of a non-negative linear combination, minus a linear term), so a projected-gradient / SLSQP solve on the simplex returns the unique global optimum — no local-optima risk. Regularization against degenerate corner solutions: add a Dirichlet(alpha0=1.5) log-prior penalty +(alpha0-1) sum_k ln w_k (ridge-toward-equal in the simplex interior) and a floor w_k >= 0.02 so a variant that merely got lucky on a short window cannot grab all mass. Cold-start (window with < N_min=8 target earthquakes, so the score is noise): FALL BACK to w = e_{V0} (all mass on the base), i.e. the pool degenerates to the reference exactly. The pinned-base guarantee plus the floor gives the fail-safe: in-sample log-score(pool) >= log-score(V0) always, and out-of-sample the only way to lose is weight-estimation variance, bounded by the Dirichlet shrinkage. This is implemented in the deferred EnsembleForecaster.fit_weights_from_history, called by run_back_analysis at each refit date with the trailing scored rows.
leakage_controls: Weights are a function ONLY of issues strictly before the current refit date t0 (trailing window [t0-L,t0)); the forecast clock's assert_no_leakage already guards each component forecast's conditioning, and the stacking adds a second clock on the WEIGHTS themselves — w(t) depends only on targets that had fully resolved before t0.

Weight refit cadence is locked to the existing weekly model-refit cadence (refit_every_days=7); on intermediate days weights are HELD (like ETAS params), never peeked-ahead and never re-tuned on the day being scored.

The window that fits the weights and the window that scores the pool are DISJOINT (rolling-origin / time-series CV, never k-fold): score day s is never in the trailing window that produced w used at s. Enforced by an assertion s >= t0 for every scored issue.
Every variant is itself fit under the existing dual-catalog + cadence rules on (-inf, s) — the stacking introduces NO new fit on the future; it only reweights already-leakage-free component rates.

All four variants share the SAME cells, SAME smoothed-null floor, and SAME target binning (_bin_counts_to_cells), so a weight cannot exploit a representation mismatch — the pool difference is purely the kernel/memory variant, isolating the real signal.
Targets scored on the NON-declustered catalog (aftershocks included) exactly as the base — the weights cannot game the metric by being tuned on a declustered target the public forecast does not face.

Reproducibility: weights, the trailing window bounds, and the per-window solve are recorded per refit date in the back-analysis JSON (auditable), so a reviewer can recompute w from the same past and get the same number.
designs.avoids_equal_weight_deadend: E8-E9 failed for a STRUCTURAL reason, not a tuning one: the equal-weight pool averaged ETAS with the stationary smoothed-null and the weak Reasenberg-Jones. Those two members have rates that are NEAR-CONSTANT (null) or near-zero-signal (R-J) exactly in the cells/times where ETAS's triggering carries the skill, so a fixed 1/3 weight on each is a mandatory ~67% down-weight of the only informative term — lambda_pool = (lambda_ETAS + lambda_null + lambda_RJ)/3 shrinks the triggering spike toward the background, lowering the log-rate at the cells where earthquakes actually occur (E9: global +2.80 < ETAS +3.71; CL -0.06 < +0.036). E12 removes the failure at its root, three ways: (1) MEMBER SET is ETAS-family variants only — every member carries triggering signal, there is no dilutant to average in (the null remains only as the cold-start FLOOR via np.maximum, never as a weighted member); (2) WEIGHTS ARE LEARNED from the past log-score, so a member that dilutes is driven toward its 0.02 floor automatically — the data, not a hard-coded 1/3, sets the mix; (3) BASE PINNED + floor + Dirichlet-toward-the-base means the convex optimum is a strict superset of the reference, so the worst case is 'collapse to base', never 'worse than base by construction'. The equal-weight pool had no such floor and no base-superset property — that is the difference.
designs.eval_protocol: Authoritative test = the existing leakage-free pseudo-prospective back-analysis (run_back_analysis), run identically for the base TiledForecaster and for E12, over the SAME issue dates / cells / horizons (1,2,7 d) / thresholds (M5,6,7), multi-region (the E5 panel: JP, CL, US-CA, NZ high-seismicity + US-CE, EU-W, AU-E low + the GLOBAL whole-Earth view). Headline metric = paired IGPE of E12 over base-tiled in nats, per the SAME per-earthquake paired-difference series csep.information_gain_per_earthquake returns (so the Rhoades 2011 T-test + Wilcoxon W-test apply unchanged). PRE-REGISTERED DECISION RULE (fixed before the run, written into experiments.md E12 stub): E12 SHIPS iff, on the GLOBAL view AND in >=2 of the high-seismicity regional views, ALL hold: (a) mean paired IGPE(E12 vs base-tiled) > 0; (b) the Rhoades paired-T 95% CI on that IGPE excludes zero on the positive side (t_ci_excludes_zero with t_ci_low>0); (c) the W-test corroborates (w_pvalue < 0.05, positive median); (d) STABILITY: the per-window IGPE is positive in >= 60% of weekly windows AND the sign does not flip on the most-recent held-out quintile (temporal-stratification guard, improvement-evidence F7/F8, Koehler 2025) — a model that only wins on early data is rejected; (e) E12 does not LOSE in any low-seismicity view by more than the noise floor (mean IGPE >= -0.005), i.e. collapsing to base where there is no triggering. If (a)-(e) do not ALL hold, E12 is RECORDED AS A DEAD-END in experiments.md with the measured (likely ~0 or noise) IGPE, exactly as E9 was — no quiet shelving. Expected honest outcome given the literature: a small positive global IGPE on the order of +0.01 to +0.02 nats (Herrmann and Marzocchi 2023 +0.016+/-0.028 over best single member), quite possibly NOT significant — in which case the honest record is 'no demonstrated prospective gain over single tiled ETAS', which is itself a valid, publishable result for this product.
top_risks: The realistic gain (~+0.016 IGPE) is SMALLER than its own standard error (Herrmann and Marzocchi report +/-0.028), so with our target counts (E5: tens of M>=5 events per regional window) the paired-T CI will very likely INCLUDE zero — E12 most probably records as 'no significant gain', a dead-end by the pre-registered rule. This is the base-rate expectation, not a failure of implementation.

Weight-estimation variance: with few target earthquakes per trailing window the log-score surface is flat and w wanders, so out-of-sample the pool can underperform the base by noise. Mitigation = base-pinning + 0.02 floor + Dirichlet(1.5) shrinkage + N_min=8 cold-start fallback to all-base; without these the fail-safe breaks.

Variant collinearity: V0-V3 are all ETAS on the same tiles, so their rate fields are highly correlated; the stacking has little orthogonal signal to exploit and the realized gain may be ~0 even when each variant is individually sensible. This is honest (the pool can only help where members genuinely disagree) but caps the upside hard.

Look-good-retrospectively trap: fitting weights on the WHOLE history (not rolling-past) would manufacture a positive IGPE that evaporates prospectively — exactly the EarthquakeNPP leakage failure (F3). The disjoint trailing-window + s>=t0 assertion is the only thing preventing it; it must be enforced, not assumed.

Multiple-comparison / region-shopping: testing 8 views inflates the chance one shows a spurious significant win. The decision rule fixes this by requiring the GLOBAL view PLUS >=2 high-seismicity views jointly (not any-one-view), and by pre-registering the views before the run.

Cost: four variant fits per refit (vs one) roughly 4x the back-analysis MLE cost; tractable only because E4's recondition/cadence holds (fit weekly, recondition daily). If a variant is reconditioned but its altered max_parent_days changes the parent set, recondition must rebuild the pairs for that variant or the held conditioning silently diverges — verify each variant's recondition honors its own cutoffs.

The smoothed-null floor (np.maximum) is applied to every variant identically; if it dominates in quiet cells, all four variants become equal there and the pool trivially equals the base in low-seismicity views (gain exactly 0) — correct and honest, but it means the ONLY place E12 can win is active high-seismicity cells, narrowing where a real gain can even appear.

The quantitative prior for E12's expected (small, possibly non-significant) gain.

Marzocchi, Zechar & Jordan (2012), BSSA 102(6):2574 — BMA / Bayes-factor / inverse-log-likelihood score weighting framework, applied to the RELM California prospective test; the operational basis for score-weighting over equal-weighting.

Marzocchi et al. (2023), GJI 234(3):2501 — OEF-Italy 10-yr operational score-model-averaging ensemble of ETAS+ETES+STEP; precedent that the production pool is ETAS-FAMILY variants, not heterogeneous models.

Yao, Vehtari, Simpson & Gelman (2018), Bayesian Analysis 13(3):917 — stacking of predictive distributions via held-out log-score on the simplex; the out-of-sample weighting method E12 uses (vs in-sample BMA posteriors).

Rhoades et al. (2011), Acta Geophysica 59:728 (doi:10.2478/s11600-011-0013-5) — IGPE in nats + the paired-T test on per-earthquake log-rate differences; already implemented in eval/csep.py and unchanged for E12.

CAOS_SEISMIC docs/improvement-evidence.md F1/F6 (deep-research 2026-06-17) — score-weighted ETAS-family ensembles are the ONLY proven prospective lever, small (+0.016 IGPE); horizon-specific weighting is real because no single model dominates all horizons.

synthesis.final_design_summary:
E12 = a score-weighted convex stacking ensemble of 3 ETAS-FAMILY VARIANTS (V0 base + V1 short-memory + V2 long-memory), combined by the existing EnsembleForecaster linear opinion pool, with a SINGLE GLOBAL weight vector learned from the rolling strictly-past Poisson log-score and shrunk hard toward the V0 vertex. This is the convergent core of all four expert designs; the disagreements (4-5 member zoos, per-regime weight simplices, anisotropic/finite-fault kernels, b-value perturbation) are deliberately DISCARDED as over-engineered or beyond our data budget.

The decisive constraint is data sparsity (Design 2's dominant point, corroborated by Design 4): M>=5 events are few (E9 recorded ~248 global per 30-day window, single digits per region), the literature prior is +0.016+/-0.028 IGPE (CI already straddles zero, Herrmann & Marzocchi 2023), and we fit K-1 free weights at ~20-30 events per weight. With K=3 we fit only 2 free weights globally; that is the largest pool the data honestly supports. We do NOT fit per-regime weights (Design 3) — that instantly blows the event budget. We do NOT add V3 (b/Mc perturbation) or V4 (finer tiling) or V5 (anisotropic kernel) in the initial pool: they are near-collinear with V0 (all tiled ETAS), and Design 3/4 themselves flag the anisotropic azimuth as a static PB2002 proxy whose gain is a lower bound. V3/V4 are stretch members admitted only if the holdout log-score strictly improves AND the weight stays in the simplex interior.

The structural anti-dilution guarantee (the E8-E9 fix) is shared by all four designs and is non-negotiable: (1) every pool member is a tiled-ETAS triggering model — the smoothed null enters ONLY as each member's mu(x,y) background and the downstream cold-start floor (np.maximum), NEVER as a weighted component; (2) weights are LEARNED from the past log-score, so a useless member is driven to its floor; (3) V0 is pinned and the weights are L2/Dirichlet-shrunk toward the e_0=(1,0,0) vertex, so the convex optimum is provably >= base tiled ETAS in-sample and degrades to exactly base on sparse/quiet holdouts. The honest expectation is a SMALL (+0.01..+0.02 nats) gain that may not be significant — pre-registered as a possible no-ship.

The objective fitted is the EXACT same Poisson log-score that backanalysis reports as IGPE (csep.poisson_joint_log_likelihood / information_gain_per_earthquake), so we directly optimize held-out information gain, not a proxy.

variants_to_implement.name: V0_base_tiled (anchor, MANDATORY, pinned vertex)

It is component k=0, the reference to beat, and the L2/Dirichlet shrinkage target. Including it makes the pool a strict superset of base: the convex optimum is >= base in-sample by putting all mass on V0.
